acceptedAnswer.text: A board foot is a unit of volume used to measure lumber. One board foot equals a piece of wood 1 inch thick, 12 inches wide, and 12 inches long (144 cubic inches). It is the standard unit for pricing hardwood lumber in the United States.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate board feet?

acceptedAnswer.text: Board feet = Thickness (inches) × Width (inches) × Length (feet) ÷ 12. For example, a board that is 1 inch thick, 6 inches wide, and 8 feet long is 1 × 6 × 8 ÷ 12 = 4 board feet.

The fraction notation (4/4, 5/4, 6/4, 8/4, etc.) describes rough lumber thickness in quarter inches. 4/4 means four quarters or 1 inch thick, 5/4 means 1.25 inches, 6/4 means 1.5 inches, 8/4 means 2 inches, 12/4 means 3 inches, and 16/4 means 4 inches.

mainEntity.name: How much does a board foot cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: Board foot prices vary widely by species and grade. Common softwoods like pine cost $2–$5 per board foot. Popular hardwoods: red oak $4–$8, hard maple $5–$10, cherry $6–$12, walnut $8–$16, and exotic species can exceed $20 per board foot. Prices depend on grade, region, and market conditions.

mainEntity.name: What's the difference between nominal and actual lumber sizes?

acceptedAnswer.text: Nominal size is the name used at the lumber yard (e.g., 2×4), while actual size is the true dimension after drying and planing. A nominal 2×4 actually measures 1.5 × 3.5 inches. However, board feet for dimensional lumber are typically calculated using the nominal dimensions.

mainEntity.name: How many board feet in a 2×4×8?

acceptedAnswer.text: A 2×4×8 contains 5.33 board feet. The calculation is: 2 inches × 4 inches × 8 feet ÷ 12 = 5.33 BF. Note that board feet for dimensional lumber use the nominal (named) dimensions, not the actual dimensions.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Board Feet

A board foot is the standard unit for measuring and pricing hardwood lumber in the United States. One board foot equals 144 cubic inches of wood — a piece 1 inch thick, 12 inches wide, and 1 foot long.

The formula:

Board Feet = Thickness (in) × Width (in) × Length (ft) ÷ 12

Key points:

  • Thickness and width are measured in inches
  • Length is measured in feet
  • For rough hardwood, use the actual rough thickness (4/4 = 1", 8/4 = 2", etc.)
  • For dimensional lumber (2×4, 2×6, etc.), board feet use the nominal dimensions
  • Multiply by the number of pieces for total board feet

Example: A 8/4 walnut board that is 6 inches wide and 8 feet long:

2" × 6" × 8' ÷ 12 = 8.00 board feet
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

What is a board foot?

A board foot (BF) is a unit of volume used to measure and price lumber, especially hardwood. One board foot equals 144 cubic inches — equivalent to a piece of wood 1 inch thick, 12 inches wide, and 12 inches (1 foot) long. It is the standard pricing unit at hardwood lumber yards across the United States.

How do you calculate board feet?

Use the formula: Thickness (inches) × Width (inches) × Length (feet) ÷ 12 = Board Feet. For example, a board that is 1" thick, 8" wide, and 10' long contains 1 × 8 × 10 ÷ 12 = 6.67 board feet. For multiple pieces of the same size, multiply the result by the quantity.

What does 4/4 lumber mean?

The quarter notation (4/4, 5/4, 6/4, 8/4, etc.) describes rough hardwood lumber thickness in quarter-inch increments. 4/4 ("four quarter") means the wood was sawn to 1 inch thick. 5/4 is 1.25 inches, 6/4 is 1.5 inches, 8/4 is 2 inches, 12/4 is 3 inches, and 16/4 is 4 inches. After surfacing (planing), the board will be slightly thinner — for example, 4/4 lumber surfaces to about 13/16 inch.

How much does a board foot cost?

Prices vary widely by species, grade, and region. Typical ranges: Poplar $3–$6/BF, Red Oak $4–$8/BF, White Oak $5–$10/BF, Hard Maple $5–$10/BF, Cherry $6–$12/BF, Walnut $8–$16/BF, Purpleheart $10–$15/BF, and Ebony $25+/BF. Thicker stock (8/4 and above) typically costs more per board foot than 4/4. FAS (First and Seconds) grade commands the highest prices.

What's the difference between nominal and actual lumber sizes?

Nominal size is the name given to dimensional lumber at the store (e.g., 2×4), while the actual size is smaller due to drying and planing. A 2×4 actually measures 1.5" × 3.5". A 1×6 is actually 0.75" × 5.5". For board foot calculations on dimensional lumber, the nominal dimensions are traditionally used. For rough hardwood, the actual rough thickness (4/4, 8/4, etc.) is used since it hasn't been planed.

How many board feet in a 2×4×8?

A 2×4×8 contains 5.33 board feet. Using the formula with nominal dimensions: 2" × 4" × 8' ÷ 12 = 5.33 BF. Note that lumber yards sell dimensional lumber (2×4, 2×6, etc.) by the linear foot, not by the board foot. Board foot pricing is primarily used for hardwood lumber.

sections.title: What Is the Board Foot Calculator?
sections.body: The Board Foot Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your thickness, width, length, you get instant results including board feet per piece, total board feet, total cost. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting board feet per piece right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your thickness and need to find the right board feet per piece. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Thickness (inches)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your thickness — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: width, length, quantity, price per board foot. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your board feet per piece, total board feet, total cost. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your thickness before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both board feet per piece and total board feet — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your price per board foot, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.

faq.question: Is the Board Foot Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What thickness should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate thickness value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

faq.question: Can I use this calculator on my phone?
faq.answer: Yes, the Board Foot Calculator is fully responsive and works on any device — phone, tablet, or desktop. No app download needed.

faq.question: What does board feet per piece mean in my results?
faq.answer: The board feet per piece value represents the primary output of the calculation based on your inputs. Use it as a baseline figure for your practical calculation planning and decision-making.
